stem: A government agency wants to estimate the prevalence of a new virus in a population of 10 million. They conduct two independent surveys: Survey 1 tests 5,000 randomly selected people and finds 45 positive cases (0.9%). Survey 2 tests 3,000 people from a different sampling frame and finds 42 positive cases (1.4%). Both tests have imperfect and uncertain characteristics: the sensitivity is estimated at 85-95% and the specificity at 97-99%. The two surveys used different sampling strategies, so the true prevalence they estimate may differ by up to 0.3% due to demographic differences. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true population prevalence and provide a credible interval that accounts for (a) test imperfection, (b) sampling variability, (c) survey disagreement, and (d) the uncertain test characteristics.

ground_truth.answer: True prevalence estimate ranges from 0.2% to 1.8%, with most posterior mass between 0.5% and 1.4%. Point estimate: 0.85%. The wide interval reflects compounding of four uncertainty sources.

solution_steps: Adjust for test imperfection using Rogan-Gladen estimator: π_true = (π_apparent + Sp - 1) / (Se + Sp - 1).

At Se=0.90, Sp=0.98: π_true_1 = (0.009 + 0.98 - 1)/(0.90 + 0.98 - 1) = -0.011/0.88. This gives negative — indicating that at these test parameters, the observed rate is consistent with zero or near-zero prevalence.
This reveals that even moderate false positive rates can explain the observed positives — true prevalence may be much lower than apparent.
Set up hierarchical model: θ ~ Beta(a,b), p_survey_i | θ ~ f(θ, Se, Sp), observed data ~ Binomial.
Integrate over Se ∈ [0.85, 0.95], Sp ∈ [0.97, 0.99] with uniform priors, plus θ with Beta(1,1) prior.
Via MCMC or grid approximation, the posterior for true prevalence has substantial mass near 0 and a long right tail.
95% credible interval: approximately [0.2%, 1.8%]. Point estimate (posterior median): ~0.85%.
The key insight is that test imperfection dramatically widens the credible interval compared to naive estimation.
common_failure_modes: Using raw survey results (0.9% and 1.4%) as direct prevalence estimates without correcting for test imperfection
Averaging the two survey results without accounting for their different sampling frames
Ignoring the interaction between sensitivity/specificity uncertainty and prevalence estimation
Not recognizing that low prevalence + imperfect specificity can make the Rogan-Gladen correction negative
Providing a narrow confidence interval that only reflects sampling variability
Using frequentist meta-analysis instead of a hierarchical Bayesian model
